title: The Ultimate Guide to Information Discernment
subtitle: Navigating Truth in the Age of Information Overload — A Curated Preview
author: Knowledge Exchange Series
footerRight: For Educational Use Only — Not for Resale

biases.name: Confirmation Bias
biases.description: We instinctively seek information that confirms our existing beliefs, creating invisible walls around our worldview.

biases.name: Anchoring Effect
biases.description: The first piece of information we encounter disproportionately influences all subsequent judgments — even when it's wrong.

biases.name: Availability Heuristic
biases.description: We overestimate the probability of events that come easily to mind, typically those that are recent, dramatic, or emotionally charged.

sharingChecklist: Have I verified this information through at least two independent, credible sources?
Am I sharing this because it is true, or because it confirms what I already believe?
Could sharing this cause harm to a person, group, or institution — even if it's technically correct?
Would I be comfortable defending this information in a professional or academic setting?
Am I emotionally triggered right now? If yes, I should wait 24 hours before sharing.

truthSandwich.name: Lead with the Truth
truthSandwich.description: Start with the verified fact, clearly and simply stated.

truthSandwich.name: Mention the Myth
truthSandwich.description: Briefly reference the false claim — but only after establishing the truth.

truthSandwich.name: Explain the Fallacy
truthSandwich.description: Clarify why the myth is wrong and what mechanism makes it persuasive.

truthSandwich.name: Reinforce the Truth
truthSandwich.description: Close by restating the verified fact with supporting evidence.

selfAssessment: Can I name at least 3 specific news sources I regularly consult, along with their political leanings?
When I encounter a surprising statistic, do I check the original study or dataset before accepting it?
Do I actively seek out perspectives that challenge my core beliefs at least once a week?
Can I explain the difference between an ad hominem attack and a legitimate critique?
Have I ever changed a strongly held opinion based on new evidence in the last 12 months?

tableOfContents.title: 2. Six Cognitive Biases That Shape Your Reality
